time.night: Locked. A candle burning in the dark before the altar. Light in an empty church is either sacred or suspicious.

attribute.EMPATHY: You feel the weight of every private grief that's passed through this room. It's considerable. You carry it without staggering.
attribute.CHARISMA: The priest opens up because something in your manner says you're worth the conversation. You don't disagree.
attribute.INTUITION: A place built for peace is currently not peaceful. Something brought someone here today that isn't on the schedule.

gender.male: The priest greets you with the careful warmth reserved for unfamiliar men. You respond with the careful honesty that earns the second conversation.
gender.female: The congregation makes room immediately. A woman entering a church alone is assumed to have every reason in the world to be there.

mosque.default: A place of order in a city that has very little. The community here watches out for its own. You're going to need them to watch out for you too.

time.morning: Fajr concluded, the mosque settling into its morning quiet. The men who pray before dawn are the ones worth knowing.
time.afternoon: Zuhr draws the midday congregation. Business of the community conducted in the margins of devotion.
time.evening: Maghrib as the light fades. The community gathering at the end of the working day. Loose ends get discussed here.
time.night: Isha, then silence. The mosque closes on the day with more dignity than the rest of the city manages.

attribute.EMPATHY: You enter with respect and leave with more than you brought. The community reads sincerity. You mean it.
attribute.CHARISMA: Community elders acknowledge you with the measured openness they extend to anyone who carries themselves without arrogance.
attribute.INTUITION: The welcome is genuine and the atmosphere is calm. The tension underneath it is neither.

gender.male: You remove your shoes, speak little, and let the space speak to you first.
gender.female: You're directed to the women's section with courtesy. The conversation there is entirely different and significantly more useful.

synagogue.default: History in every corner and a community that's survived enough to know how to read a stranger. They're reading you now.

time.morning: Morning minyan. The committed few who show up before the day starts. Short on excuses, long on information.
time.afternoon: Study sessions and committee business. The administrative life of the community running parallel to the spiritual one.
time.evening: Shabbat or weekday evening service. The week's events processed through the liturgy and the conversation afterward.
time.night: Locked. The Ner Tamid burns without end. The eternal light is the only honest thing still running at this hour.

attribute.EMPATHY: The community's depth of history is not academic here — it's lived. You meet it without flinching.
attribute.MEMORY: The archives in the back room go back further than any police record. The shammes knows where they are. So do you.
attribute.INTELLIGENCE: A building that is simultaneously a house of worship, a community centre, and a library has more answers per square foot than most precincts.

gender.male: You're handed a kippah and a siddur and welcomed into the rhythm of the place. The conversation comes after the service.
gender.female: The rebbetzin finds you before you've oriented yourself. She's been watching the door. So have you.

office.default: The standard-issue office. Fluorescent lights, bad coffee, and the persistent hum of people doing things they'd rather not explain.

time.morning: Everyone's performing competence before the first coffee. The performance has tells.
time.afternoon: Midday fog. The afternoon is where the careful discipline of the morning starts to crack.
time.evening: Most desks dark by six. The ones still lit are either dedicated or desperate. Probably both.
time.night: Empty floor, one light burning. That's not the cleaner and it's not overtime.

attribute.INTELLIGENCE: You read the workspace in thirty seconds flat — who sits where, what's on their screens, whose desk has been tidied too recently.
attribute.PERCEPTION: A sticky note face-down on a keyboard. A filing cabinet drawer that doesn't close flush. Small things. Important ones.
attribute.STEALTH: You move through the open plan like a shadow in a building full of people too busy to look sideways.
attribute.EMPATHY: The stress in this place has a texture. You can feel it before anyone speaks.

gender.male: You carry the authority of a man who belongs here. Nobody checks the credentials of someone who looks certain.
gender.female: You move through the space with the focus of someone who came here for a specific reason. You did.

gym.default: Iron and sweat and the grim religion of people trying to outrun who they are. You've got your own reasons for being here.

time.morning: Pre-dawn crowd. Either very disciplined, very guilty, or they can't sleep. Could be all three.
time.afternoon: Midday regulars. Less noise, better angles. You use both.
time.evening: After-work crowd working out the day's frustrations on equipment that doesn't talk back. Except to you.
time.night: The diehards. The ones who train when the rest of the city gives up. You know what drives them. You've been driven by worse.

attribute.STRENGTH: You rack the bar nobody's touched and feel the familiar burn of the only thing that never lied to you.
attribute.STAMINA: You set a pace that clears the cardio floor around you. That's not an accident.
attribute.INTELLIGENCE: You study the mechanics of every person in the room. Most of them are performing. One of them isn't.
attribute.AGILITY: You move through the space like the floor was designed for you. Because this kind of floor always was.

gender.male: Two regulars offer you a spot. The gym's social codes are blunt and readable. You use that.
gender.female: You take the weights nobody's moved today and ignore the three people who stopped what they were doing. The job doesn't wait for an audience.

hospital.default: The place where the city's luck runs out. You've been here before. The smell never changes. Neither do the lies.

time.morning: Ward rounds and morning admissions. The machine of care cranking into motion. Your window to move with it.
time.afternoon: Visiting hours open the building to the world. You're part of the world. You use that.
time.evening: Families saying goodnight. The corridors thinner now, the remaining faces more accountable.
time.night: Night staff and emergencies. Fewer distractions, tighter watch. The work gets harder at night. So do you.

attribute.EMPATHY: The weight of this place settles on you the moment you walk in. You carry it. You've been carrying heavier.
attribute.INTELLIGENCE: The chart, the ward allocation, the medication schedule — you read between the lines until the lines stop hiding things.
attribute.STAMINA: The hospital never stops. You haven't either. It's a fair trade.
attribute.PERCEPTION: Someone in this building is where they shouldn't be. You identify the floor before you've left the lobby.

gender.male: You walk the ward with the authority of a man running late for rounds. Nobody stops late authority.
gender.female: A nurse offers to help. You take the offer and redirect it into the corridor you actually needed.

park.default: Green space and dead ends. The city pretends parks are peaceful. You know what gets buried under the grass.

time.morning: Dog walkers and joggers on their circuits. The regulars know each other and they know what's out of place.
time.afternoon: Families and office workers taking lunch. The park at its most populated and its least guarded.
time.evening: Golden hour. Long shadows and the city softening just long enough to look like it didn't do anything wrong.
time.night: Empty benches and yellow lamp circles. The park at night belongs to the city's other population.

attribute.PERCEPTION: A discarded item near the second bench that four hundred people walked past today. You stop.
attribute.STAMINA: You run the perimeter. Once for the exercise, twice for the inventory, three times because something's wrong and you need to find it.
attribute.INTUITION: The park looks exactly like a park should. That's exactly why you don't trust it.
attribute.CHARISMA: The regular on the bench has been watching the path for three hours. You sit down and within five minutes they're talking.

gender.male: You walk the main path and let the park's usual population decide you're not their problem.
gender.female: Daylight, main path, aware of every entrance. Not paranoia — procedure.

restaurant.default: Hot food and cold truths. Every table in a restaurant is a negotiation. You want to know who's losing theirs.

time.morning: Breakfast crowd with nowhere urgent to be. Loose with it, honest about it.
time.afternoon: The lunchtime rush — overworked, underpaid, and spilling their day to anyone who's listening. That's you.
time.evening: Dinner service. Soft lighting over hard conversations. The city conducting its private business in a public place.
time.night: Kitchen winding down. Last round of drinks. The hour when people say what they've been holding since the appetiser.

attribute.CHARISMA: The host gives you the corner booth before you ask for it. You've given them no reason not to.
attribute.PERCEPTION: Table eight hasn't touched their food in twenty minutes. They're not here for the food.
attribute.EMPATHY: The staff are running on fumes and professional courtesy. You read the difference and don't add to the weight.
attribute.INTELLIGENCE: The reservation under a name that doesn't match the credit card at the next table. You make a note.

gender.male: Solo table, back to the wall, eyes on the room. The oldest habit you have and the most useful.
gender.female: The server is attentive in ways you don't need. You redirect it into the conversation you do.

24hr Access.default: Round-the-clock access. Whoever owns this building trusts that nobody interesting shows up after midnight. They're wrong.

gender.male: The lock doesn't care what time it is. Neither do you.
gender.female: Twenty-four hours means twenty-four ways in. You've already picked the one that suits you.

24hr Security.default: A guard on every shift, a camera on every corner. The building that never sleeps is hiding something worth staying awake for.

gender.male: You clock the rotation. Three minutes between passes. More than enough.
gender.female: The overnight guard straightens up when you walk past. You note the camera angle they just created for you.

3D Theaters.default: Three dimensions of distraction. The kind of spectacle designed to keep a thousand eyes pointing the wrong way.

gender.male: You skip the glasses and watch the crowd instead. More interesting than the screen.
gender.female: You take a seat on the aisle and spend two hours observing people who think the dark makes them invisible.

ATM.default: A machine that knows exactly who was here and when. The bank will tell you. Eventually.

gender.male: Cash leaves no trail. Which is precisely why the trail starts here.
gender.female: The timestamp on the last transaction is three minutes before the incident. You photograph the screen.

ATM Lobby.default: A glass box of transaction records and overhead cameras. Everything in here is evidence of something.

gender.male: You stand at the machine long enough to read the reflection in the screen. Someone followed you in.
gender.female: Small space, good lighting, three cameras. You use all of that before you make your move.

Ablution Area.default: The ritual wash before prayer. Clean hands, clean conscience — or at least the attempt at one. You've seen what waits on the other side of both.

gender.male: You observe the space with respect and note the detail most visitors miss.
gender.female: The women's section is quieter, and the quiet holds more than most people realise.

Activity Room.default: Scheduled joy. Craft tables, folding chairs, and the resigned optimism of people making the best of their situation.

gender.male: You take a seat at the back and let the room come to you. It always does.
gender.female: You join the activity without being asked. In five minutes you know everyone's name. In ten you know why you came.

Admin Office.default: The engine room of the building's paperwork. Files, schedules, and the one record that shouldn't still be here.

gender.male: You lean over the counter with the relaxed authority of someone expecting cooperation. They cooperate.
gender.female: The admin clerk looks up and finds someone who clearly knows what they're looking for. You do.

Administration.default: Bureaucracy in its purest form. Every answer is in here somewhere under a layer of procedure and coffee rings.

gender.male: You use the phrase 'urgent compliance review' and doors open that weren't even closed.
gender.female: You ask exactly the right question and watch the administrator's hands go still for half a second.

Administrative Office.default: Schedules, payroll, and the institutional memory of everyone who ever worked here. Someone filed something they shouldn't have. You're going to find it.

gender.male: The staff assumes you have clearance before you show any. You don't correct them.
gender.female: You sit across from the desk with the look of someone who already read the file. The administrator starts talking.

Air Station.default: Compressed air and the honest work of keeping vehicles road-worthy. Simple. Clean. Unless you know what was parked here recently.

gender.male: You check your tyres and check the lot. Both tell you something useful.
gender.female: The pressure gauge is still set from the last vehicle. You note the reading and do the math.

Alignment Rack.default: The machine that straightens what the road bends. One of these left a very specific mark on a very specific vehicle. You know which one.

gender.male: You know alignment work. You also know when the rack's been used on something that didn't come in through the front door.
gender.female: The technician explains the process. Halfway through you ask the question they weren't prepared for.

Alterations Area.default: Needles and thread and the patient reconstruction of things that no longer fit. You know that feeling.

gender.male: You hand over the jacket. The tailor's hands find the lining before they find the hem.
gender.female: You talk about the alteration and watch what the seamstress notices about the wear patterns.

Ambulance Bay.default: The threshold between outside and inside, fast and still, breathing and not. Whatever came through here tonight, it mattered.

gender.male: You stand at the edge of the bay in scrubs that almost fit and nobody stops you.
gender.female: You walk the bay with the measured pace of someone assessing the space. Which is exactly what you are.

Anchor Store.default: The flagship tenant. The name above the door that everyone else orbits. What happens to it when the orbit breaks?

gender.male: You blend into the retail flow. Men browsing look purposeless. You are not purposeless.
gender.female: You move through the floor the way a store detective does: casually, completely, and unseen.

Arcade.default: Flashing screens and the mechanical soundtrack of games that take your money and give you points. A perfect cover for a conversation nobody should overhear.

gender.male: You feed a token into a machine you're not playing and watch the room in the screen's reflection.
gender.female: You're good at the games. People underestimate women at arcade machines. You use the surprise.

Archive Room.default: Cardboard boxes and the weight of institutional memory. Everything that ever happened here is in this room. You just have to find the right box.

gender.male: You pull the third box from the left. The dust on the lid says nobody else thought to look here.
gender.female: You read filing systems like a second language. This one has a gap in it that wasn't there by accident.

Archives.default: The official record of everything that officially happened. The unofficial record is what you're actually after.

gender.male: You request the file under a purpose that's technically true. The archivist hands it over without question.
gender.female: You know exactly which year, which box, which shelf. The archivist asks how. You smile and don't answer.

Armory.default: Locked, logged, and accounted for. Except for the one that isn't.

gender.male: You read the inventory sheet like a ledger. The arithmetic is clean. The numbers are not.
gender.female: Every weapon has a sign-out signature. You're looking for the one that was signed back in under a different name.

Art Gallery.default: Canvas and pretension stretched over something worth a great deal more than the asking price. The money in these rooms never answers where it came from.

gender.male: You stand in front of the most expensive piece and wait for the person who's here for the same reason you are.
gender.female: The curator explains the provenance at length. You let them. Provenance stories are where the lies live.

Art Room.default: Pencils, paint, and the unguarded expression of people who stopped editing themselves. The most honest room in the building.

gender.male: You pick up a pencil and sketch something that looks like nothing. Two people in the room stop watching you.
gender.female: You sit at the table and work alongside them. Side by side is the easiest way to have a conversation that matters.

Art Studio.default: The private work of someone who thinks the canvas doesn't talk back. It does. You read it.

gender.male: You study the unfinished piece on the easel. The subject is more specific than the artist intended.
gender.female: You ask what they're working on. Artists explain. In the explanation is the detail you needed.

Arts Center.default: Performance spaces and creative infrastructure. A lot of people passing through, a lot of off-the-books schedules. Useful.

gender.male: You walk it like a patron between shows. Nobody questions the man who looks like he belongs to a board.
gender.female: You introduce yourself as an arts journalist. Four people tell you things in the next twenty minutes.

Assembly Line.default: Identical repetition. Every product the same, every motion the same — which makes the one deviation impossible to miss.

gender.male: You walk the line slow. The workers clock you. One of them clocks you twice.
gender.female: You take a position at the end of the run and watch every unit coming off it. One in eight is different.

Athletic Fields.default: Open ground and clear sight lines. No cover, no corners. Whatever you're here to do, you're doing it in daylight.

gender.male: You cross the field at a jog. It looks like exercise. It's reconnaissance.
gender.female: You stretch at the edge of the field and take a full visual sweep before you move.

Attic Storage.default: What people keep above the ceiling tells you more about them than anything on the floors below.

gender.male: You pull the hatch and go up without being asked. What's up here wasn't meant to be found.
gender.female: You note what's been moved recently. Attic dust keeps a perfect record.

Attorney Meeting Rooms.default: Privileged conversations behind soundproofed walls. The law says you can't listen. The law isn't always in the room.

gender.male: You take the chair outside and wait. Lawyers always come back for something they forgot.
gender.female: You make yourself useful to the paralegal. Paralegal's see everything and are rarely thanked for it.

Audiobook Section.default: Stories told aloud for people who'd rather not read between the lines. You always read between the lines.

gender.male: You select a title you've already heard and spend the time studying the person three rows over who's listening to something else entirely.
gender.female: The librarian recommends something. You accept it and ask what else they've had requests for lately.

Auditorium.default: A stage and a thousand seats. Someone in here is performing and it isn't the act on the programme.

gender.male: You take the back row. Every exit is visible from the back row.
gender.female: You sit centre and watch the wings. Stage managers know where the bodies are. Figuratively speaking.

Auto Buying Program.default: Corporate fleet purchasing. The paperwork looks legitimate until you check the delivery addresses.

gender.male: You ask about volume discounts. The sales rep opens up the back-office numbers to impress you.
gender.female: You ask to see the corporate account terms. The rep hesitates before showing you. That hesitation is the answer.

Auto Shop.default: Grease and diagnostics and the mechanical truth that vehicles don't lie even when their owners do.

gender.male: You talk the work without translating. The mechanic relaxes. Relaxed mechanics answer questions they wouldn't otherwise.
gender.female: You know more than the tech expected. They stop explaining and start talking.

Automotive Bay.default: A vehicle on the lift tells you where it's been. The undercarriage doesn't lie.

gender.male: You step under with the tech and look where they're not looking.
gender.female: You ask the tech to walk you through what they found. In the walk-through, you find what they missed.

Backup Generators.default: Power that doesn't depend on the grid. Whatever this building is protecting, it's protecting it from the dark too.

gender.male: You check the fuel level. Last topped off forty-eight hours ago. That's consistent with someone who expected to need it.
gender.female: The generator specs are posted on the wall. You photograph them. Capacity tells you how long they planned to run.

Backyard.default: Private open space. The neighbour can see it. The security camera can't. That asymmetry is useful.

gender.male: You climb the fence from the alley. The yard tells you who lives here without you going inside.
gender.female: You assess the yard from the lane. The garden tells a story about what the front door won't.

Bakery.default: Flour, heat, and people who arrive before dawn. The bakery shift starts when most of the city is still lying to itself in bed.

gender.male: You take a coffee at the counter and let the early-morning crew talk. They always do.
gender.female: The baker tells you about the regular who didn't come in this morning. That's unusual enough to matter.

Ballroom.default: Grand architecture built for performances that look social. The real conversations happen near the edges of the room, not the centre.

gender.male: You position yourself at the bar with a clear view of every table. You don't drink. You watch.
gender.female: You're asked to dance twice. Both times you use the two minutes on the floor to see what you couldn't from the side.

Band Room.default: Soundproofed and unsupervised. The best conversations in a school happen where the teachers can't hear them.

gender.male: You push the door open and catch the tail end of something that wasn't a rehearsal.
gender.female: You sit in on the session. Music is the best cover for a conversation you're not supposed to be having.

Bank Branch.default: Money changes hands here and somewhere in the transaction log is the move you've been looking for.

gender.male: You join the queue and study the tellers. One of them is too careful. That's as suspicious as careless.
gender.female: You ask to speak to the branch manager about a discrepancy. The manager's face before they compose it tells you everything.

Banking Services.default: Financial products and the paper trail that comes with them. Someone's using this counter for something the prospectus doesn't mention.

gender.male: You ask for a service you don't need. The process gives you access to the back office.
gender.female: You enquire about wire transfer limits. The adviser explains more than the question required.

Banquet Hall.default: A room built for celebration and the kind of deals that get toasted without being named.

gender.male: You position yourself near the service door. Staff see everything and get forgotten immediately.
gender.female: You move between tables with the ease of a professional host. Nobody questions whose guest you are.

Bar.default: Liquid honesty. The bar is where people say what they mean when they've stopped trying not to.

gender.male: You order something cheap and make it last. A man nursing a drink at a bar is invisible.
gender.female: You order, you listen, and when the man next to you wants to talk you let him. He tells you more than he'll remember telling you.

Bar Area.default: The gravitational centre of any room with a licence. Everything worth knowing gets said within ten feet of a bar.

gender.male: You lean against it and face the room. Everything in here is moving toward you.
gender.female: You take a stool with your back not quite to the wall and your eyes moving. Nobody takes up position like that accidentally.

Bar Lounge.default: Softer lighting and harder drinks than the main floor. The bar lounge is where the real meetings happen.

gender.male: You claim the corner sofa before seven. It's the only seat that sees the whole room.
gender.female: You're approached before your drink arrives. That's three minutes of intelligence before you've said a word.

Basement.default: Below ground and below scrutiny. The basement is where buildings keep what they don't want to explain.

gender.male: You find the stairs before you find the lift. The basement is never on the directory.
gender.female: You locate the utility access at the end of the ground-floor corridor. The way down is never the one they show you.

Basketball Court.default: Concrete, screech of sneakers, and the unguarded conversation of people who left their filter in the locker room.

gender.male: You run a few sets. Physical effort is the fastest way to be accepted somewhere without a badge.
gender.female: You watch from the bench and wait for the moment the game ends. Post-game is when the talking starts.

Battery Charging.default: Power going in and information coming out. Every charge cycle logs a device to a time and a location.

gender.male: You plug in and wait. Waiting is most of the job. The rest is knowing what you waited for.
gender.female: You note which bays are occupied. What's charging here at this hour doesn't belong to the building.

Battery Room.default: Racks of stored power humming in the dark. The building runs on these when everything else fails. Including the security system.

gender.male: You find the main disconnect and note the sequence. When the lights go out, you want to know the order.
gender.female: You photograph the maintenance log. The last service entry is three weeks overdue. That was deliberate.

Beer Garden.default: Open air, cold pints, and people who've loosened everything including their discretion. You prefer this to the inside.

gender.male: You take the table closest to the conversation you came to hear and look like you're enjoying the weather.
gender.female: You join the table next to the one you want. People include the woman next to them. They don't include the man.

Bicycle Storage.default: Bikes locked to racks in a room nobody visits unless they need to. Which makes it a useful room for other things.

gender.male: You lock your bike at the far end and stay in the room a little longer than it takes to do that.
gender.female: You act like you've mislocked your cable. Forty-five seconds is enough time to cover the room.

Bike Rack.default: A timestamp for every commuter. The bike that's been here since Tuesday belongs to someone who stopped commuting.

gender.male: You check the lock type. High-end lock on a low-end bike. Or the other way around. Both are interesting.
gender.female: You note which racks are used daily and which one hasn't moved. One hasn't moved in four days.

Bike Room.default: Residents' bikes and their schedules, helpfully stored together in one unsupervised room.

gender.male: You note the registration stickers on the frames. Three of them match vehicles you've been looking at.
gender.female: You hold the door and exchange nods with the resident on their way out. Their departure time is what you needed.

Bike Storage.default: Unclaimed bikes become unclaimed time. Someone parked here and didn't come back. You want to know why.

gender.male: You check the wear on the pedals and tyres. This bike has been used harder than it looks.
gender.female: You photograph the lock brand and the bike's registration mark. Both lead somewhere.

Billiards Area.default: A felt table and the steady geometry of people who think in angles. Some of them think in other kinds of angles too.

gender.male: You take a cue and offer a game. The table is where business gets done in rooms like this.
gender.female: You beat the first challenger without effort. After that, people take you seriously enough to talk.

Blood Draw Station.default: The quiet transaction of taking something from someone's body and logging where it goes. The log is what interests you.

gender.male: You make the appointment and use the waiting time to understand who else is in the system.
gender.female: You ask the phlebotomist about volume — how many draws today. The answer is one more than the schedule shows.

Blood Pressure Station.default: Anyone can walk up and use it. Most people do without thinking. That means the readings tell you something about who was here.

gender.male: You use the cuff. Your reading is steady. It always is.
gender.female: You note the last reading still displayed on the screen. High enough to be significant. Recent enough to matter.

Board Room.default: Long table, leather chairs, and the space where decisions get made that the minutes never fully capture.

gender.male: You take the chair at the end that faces the door. Nobody questions who sits at the head of the table.
gender.female: You arrive before everyone else and position yourself where the conversation flows past you. It always does.

Body Shop.default: Panels and paint and the craft of making damage invisible. You're interested in the damage they made invisible recently.

gender.male: You speak the shop's language and ask about the metallic blue they mixed last week.
gender.female: You ask about repair timelines. The painter explains the last rush job without realising they've explained something else entirely.

Book Stacks.default: Rows of spines in the quiet. The book that's been pulled and replaced recently leaves a gap in the dust.

gender.male: You move through the stacks with the deliberate pace of someone who knows what they're looking for. Which you do.
gender.female: You find the gap on the shelf and pull the neighbouring volume. The margin notes answer your question.

Booking Area.default: The formal intake of someone who's had a very bad night. Every name in this log connects to something.

gender.male: You read the booking sheet upside down from across the counter. An old trick. Still works.
gender.female: You ask the desk officer about a specific booking time. They give you more than the name.

Bookstore.default: Shelves of evidence that people think and buy in patterns. One title in here is flying off the shelves for the wrong reason.

gender.male: You browse the section you didn't come for and listen to the conversation two aisles over.
gender.female: The bookseller asks if you need help. You let them recommend. People reveal themselves in what they recommend to strangers.

Bottle Service.default: Marked-up spirits and the performance of status. The table that ordered bottle service isn't here for the drinks.

gender.male: You get close enough to see who's at the table and what they're not drinking.
gender.female: You make brief eye contact with the person at the table who's working the room. They're doing the same job you are.

Bowling Alley.default: Pins and noise and the social ease of people who've stopped trying to impress each other. Conversations flow here.

gender.male: You bowl badly on purpose. Nothing makes people comfortable like beating you.
gender.female: You bowl well without announcing it. The scores create a conversation. The conversation creates an opening.

Bowling Lanes.default: Twelve lanes and twelve separate conversations all happening under the same roof. You're only interested in one.

gender.male: You take the lane next to the one you came for and let the game do the work.
gender.female: You wait for the frame to end before you ask your question. The pause between frames is when people are honest.

Box Office.default: Tickets and times. Someone was here, bought something, and the transaction was recorded. That record is yours.

gender.male: You buy a ticket for the wrong show and ask the cashier about last night's house.
gender.female: You enquire about group bookings and get the seating manifest without asking for it by name.

Break Room.default: The most honest room in any building. People say what they actually think in break rooms because they've forgotten you're listening.

gender.male: You pour a coffee and say nothing. The room does the work.
gender.female: You join the table and ask how long people have been here. Six minutes later you know the whole floor.

Building Management Office.default: Master keys, maintenance schedules, and the complaint log that's the most candid document in the building.

gender.male: You arrive with a toolbox you didn't come to use and leave with access you didn't have to ask for.
gender.female: You report a maintenance issue on a floor you needed access to. The super takes you there personally.

Bullet-Resistant Glass.default: Someone already thought about stopping what you're thinking about. The glass tells you exactly how seriously they took the threat.

gender.male: You tap the glass and calculate the grade. This isn't a standard installation. Someone spent more than the threat required.
gender.female: You note the retrofit seams. This was added after the fact. Which means something changed.

Burn Center.default: Specialized care for the kind of damage that changes everything. What ended up here, and how — that's the case.

gender.male: You show credentials to the ward nurse and make it clear you're not asking permission.
gender.female: You speak to the attending with the clinical directness of someone who belongs here. They respond in kind.

Bus Stop.default: A fixed point on a fixed route where anyone with a bus pass and nowhere to be can become invisible. Including you.

gender.male: You sit on the bench and watch the building across the road. Seven buses pass. You take the eighth.
gender.female: You check the timetable and note what was running at the relevant hour. Someone was here waiting.

Business Center.default: Printers, workstations, and the confidential documents of people who think hotel business centres are private.

gender.male: You sit at the terminal farthest from the door. The print queue on the shared printer tells you everything.
gender.female: You use the scanner and notice what the previous user forgot to clear. They forgot a lot.

Butcher.default: Precision cuts and cold storage. The butcher's block knows things about anatomy that most people would rather not.

gender.male: You know enough about the craft to ask the right questions. The butcher respects that.
gender.female: You enquire about special orders. The log of special orders is a list of interesting names.

Butler Service.default: Discreet service means the butler sees everything and is paid to forget it. You're here to help them remember.

gender.male: You speak to the butler as an equal. They appreciate it. Appreciation loosens the professional discretion.
gender.female: You ask about the guest's routine. The butler has been waiting for someone to ask.

Cable Management.default: Organized conduit running behind walls and under floors. Every wire leads somewhere. You want to know where these ones go.

gender.male: You trace the run from the server rack to the junction point. One branch doesn't appear on the building plan.
gender.female: You photograph the panel layout. The extra cable wasn't installed by the building's contractor.

Cafeteria.default: Institutional food and the democratic misery of people eating together because they have to. Best intelligence feed in the building.

gender.male: You take a tray and a seat near the loudest table. Loud tables have nothing to hide.
gender.female: You sit across from someone eating alone. Nobody eats alone in a cafeteria by preference.

Campus Security.default: Uniformed authority on a campus that assumes good faith. The gap between assumption and enforcement is where you work.

gender.male: You walk like you're in a hurry to be somewhere specific. Campus security only stops people who look lost.
gender.female: You engage the guard in a question about parking. By the end of it you have their schedule.

Cancer Center.default: Hope measured in percentages and the particular courage of people fighting a very long game. Someone here has information tied to the case.

gender.male: You sit in the waiting area. People waiting here talk to strangers. It's that kind of place.
gender.female: You speak quietly and without hurry. In a place like this, someone who listens gets trusted fast.

Canopy Lighting.default: Overhead floods that eliminate shadow. Whoever installed this didn't want anything happening in the dark. Something is happening anyway.

gender.male: You stay outside the lit perimeter and observe. The light is the border. You stay on your side.
gender.female: You walk under it without breaking stride. Confidence under good lighting is its own kind of cover.

Captain Office.default: The rank that runs the floor. Operational decisions live here, and so do the records that don't make it to the official log.

gender.male: You knock and enter in the same motion. Captains respect people who don't ask permission to be somewhere.
gender.female: You introduce yourself with the title that belongs to the job you're doing. The captain reassesses you in real time.

Car Service.default: Door-to-door and logged. Where did this vehicle go, who called it, and what time did it come back.

gender.male: You request the run history for a specific account. The dispatcher gives you more than they should.
gender.female: You ask about a regular booking under the guise of confirming a reservation. The dispatcher confirms it and tells you the rest.

Car Wash.default: Evidence removed by machine and pressure and the unsuspecting efficiency of a five-dollar wash.

gender.male: You check the drain. Water washes most things. Not everything.
gender.female: You note the vehicle in bay three. The detail work being done on it isn't standard service.

Cardiac Care.default: The unit where the body's most important failure gets managed. What someone's heart did under pressure is what you're here to understand.

gender.male: You speak to the attending under the pretence of a transfer query. The admission record is in the answer.
gender.female: You introduce yourself to the nurse coordinator and explain what you need clearly. Clarity earns faster cooperation here than rank.

Cardio Equipment.default: Treadmills and monitors and the grinding patience of people running toward nothing in particular. You run toward something.

gender.male: You take the machine with the best sight line to the room and set a pace that lets you stay for forty minutes.
gender.female: You match the pace of the person next to you. Side by side is the easiest entry to a conversation.

Caretaker Suite.default: Accommodation for the person who maintains the building and watches it sleep. The caretaker sees more than the owners.

gender.male: You knock on the caretaker's door after hours. People who work alone are glad for the company. Glad people talk.
gender.female: You ask the caretaker for help with something small. While they help, you ask the question you actually came for.

Carousel.default: Slow rotation and the particular disorientation of watching the same things come around again. You feel that way about this case.

gender.male: You take a position at the centre and let the environment circle you. Fixed point. Moving periphery.
gender.female: You ride one circuit and use the elevation to take a full visual sweep of the surrounding space.

Carrier Route.default: The delivery path that every parcel on this block follows. Somewhere on this route something was diverted.

gender.male: You walk the route in reverse. The return path is where the diversions happen.
gender.female: You speak to the postal carrier at the end of their shift. End of shift is when people say what they actually saw.

Cart Corral.default: Metal and rubber and the minimum effort of people who almost put their carts back. One of those carts was in a different location last night.

gender.male: You check the corral at the far end of the lot. The one nobody uses unless they have a reason.
gender.female: You note which corral is closest to the service entrance. Proximity to access points is rarely accidental.

Cast Room.default: Plaster and patience and the documentation of exactly how someone's body broke. The fracture pattern tells a story.

gender.male: You ask the technician about the pattern. The hesitation before they answer is the answer.
gender.female: You review the intake form under the guise of checking the chart. The mechanism of injury column has been left vague.

Cat Ward.default: Feline patients and the veterinary staff who manage them. Animals don't intimidate and neither does the room. It yields information quickly.

gender.male: You ask about the cat brought in on Tuesday. The vet checks the log before they answer. Interesting.
gender.female: You speak to the vet tech and ask about overnight admissions. One of them doesn't have a legitimate owner.

Cellar Storage.default: Below the floor and below suspicion. Cellars hold what the residents don't want on display.

gender.male: You take the stairs down with a torch you brought yourself. The building's torch has been moved.
gender.female: You note the smell before you note anything else. Temperature-controlled storage has a specific smell. This isn't it.

Change Machine.default: Coins dispensed in exchange for paper. Untraceable tender for the person who prefers their transactions off the ledger.

gender.male: You feed in a note and count what comes back. Someone's been using this machine in quantities that don't match laundry.
gender.female: You check the float indicator on the side. The machine was restocked very recently. The manager will know why.

Chapel.default: A quiet room in a building that rarely gets quiet. People come here when they have something they can't say anywhere else.

gender.male: You sit in the back pew and let the room settle. It tells you things before anyone speaks.
gender.female: You light a candle you didn't come here to light and wait. Someone comes in shortly after.

Charging Stations.default: Tethered devices and the people who can't leave without them. A charging station is a twenty-minute dwell point. That's long enough.

gender.male: You plug in and stay put. A man charging his phone is the most unthreatening thing in any public space.
gender.female: You take the station beside the one in use and ask if it's working. The person plugged in tells you more than they intended.

Check-In Kiosk.default: Self-service intake that logs every transaction and asks no questions. The questions were already answered before you arrived.

gender.male: You use the kiosk and watch what the screen holds before it resets. It holds quite a bit.
gender.female: You check in and note the last name still showing on the confirmation screen. That name is useful.

Chef's Garden.default: Herbs and fresh produce and the particular pride of a kitchen that grows what it serves. Someone in this garden knows the schedule of everyone who works here.

gender.male: You find the head gardener at six a.m. and ask a question they weren't expecting before breakfast.
gender.female: You admire the garden with genuine interest. The chef comes out personally. Chefs talk when they're proud.

Chef's Table.default: The best seat in the house and the most exposed. Everyone at the chef's table is being watched as much as they're watching.

gender.male: You request the table and spend the meal reading every person who passes through the kitchen door.
gender.female: You're seated and immediately in conversation with the chef. An hour later you know the guest list for the private event three days ago.

Child Care.default: Small children and the staff who keep them contained and happy. The adults in this room notice everything.

gender.male: You visit under the guise of an inspection. The staff are thorough about credentials. You have the right ones.
gender.female: You arrive at drop-off time and observe who doesn't collect at pick-up.

Children's Area.default: Bright colours and small voices and parents who aren't watching each other. You are.

gender.male: You take the parent bench and look like you're watching your kid. You're watching the room.
gender.female: You help a child find a book they're looking for. The parent thanks you and starts talking.

Children's Playroom.default: Supervised play in a building where the adults believe they're fully in control. They're not.

gender.male: You wait outside the door. The who-comes-and-goes at a playroom is more interesting than what's inside.
gender.female: You volunteer briefly with the activity. Volunteerism opens doors that credentials don't.

Children's Room.default: A room that holds secrets the occupant can't articulate. You speak the language anyway.

gender.male: You read the space with professional detachment. The arrangement tells a story about the adults in the house.
gender.female: You sit at the small table and ask questions a child can answer. The answers are more complete than the adults would provide.

Choir Room.default: Acoustics built for projection and the kind of conversation that carries further than people expect.

gender.male: You stand in the corridor outside. Everything said in that room arrives at the door.
gender.female: You join the practice on the pretext of an enquiry and stay for the part of the conversation that happens after.

Cigar Lounge.default: Slow smoke and slower discretion. The people in this room have decided the rules don't apply to them. That's a useful assumption to let them keep.

gender.male: You accept the offered cigar and the conversation that comes with it. Both burn a long time.
gender.female: Your presence in a cigar lounge shifts the dynamic before you've said a word. You use the shift.

City Manager.default: The person who actually runs the city while the elected officials perform running it. The real power, the real files, the real answers.

gender.male: You get five minutes by implying you're from oversight. Five minutes is all you need.
gender.female: You bypass the aide and speak directly. The city manager recognises that move and responds to it.

Classroom.default: Four walls of controlled information and the children who receive it. Something in this room's been disrupted and it wasn't the curriculum.

gender.male: You speak to the teacher after hours when the room is empty and the guard is down.
gender.female: You sit in on the session as an observer. Teachers teach differently when someone's watching. The difference is informative.

Classrooms.default: Multiple rooms, multiple sessions, multiple things happening simultaneously that nobody's keeping a full account of.

gender.male: You check which rooms are occupied and which aren't when they should be.
gender.female: You walk the corridor during change-over and note which classroom has the door closed when every other one is open.

Clerk's Office.default: The official record of official transactions. Buried in the paperwork is the transaction that wasn't meant to be official.

gender.male: You request a specific filing date. The clerk checks and you read what's adjacent to what they're showing you.
gender.female: You ask for a certified copy. While the clerk prepares it, you note the other documents on the desk.

Climate Control.default: Regulated temperature and the mechanical certainty that something in this building requires precise environmental conditions. You want to know what.

gender.male: You find the thermostat panel. The set point is lower than any human comfort range. This room isn't for humans.
gender.female: You note the humidity readout. The stored documents in here are being protected. From more than just time.

Climbing Wall.default: Vertical training and the exposure of everyone attempting it. Up a wall there's nowhere to hide your weaknesses.

gender.male: You climb the route nobody's completed today and use the height to see the whole room.
gender.female: You take an easy route and observe the people on the harder ones. Effort reveals character.

Club Level.default: Elevated access for people who've paid for the privilege of being harder to approach. You approach anyway.

gender.male: You move through the club level with the ease of someone who belongs there. The ease is the credential.
gender.female: You're admitted faster than expected. The assumption about why you're here is wrong. You let it run.

Clubhouse.default: A semi-private gathering point where the rules of the community get made and unmade. Everything consequential was agreed on here first.

gender.male: You arrive early and talk to the people setting up. They know everything before anyone else does.
gender.female: You stay late when others are leaving. The last conversation of the evening is the one that matters.

Co-Working Space.default: Hot desks and the illusion of separation. Everything here is technically private and practically public.

gender.male: You set up near the printer and let the print queue educate you.
gender.female: You introduce yourself to the person at the next desk. In a co-working space introductions are expected. The conversation is optional. You make it mandatory.

Coat Check.default: Belongings surrendered and numbered. The coat check knows what every guest brought in and when they stopped caring about getting it back.

gender.male: You retrieve a coat that isn't yours and watch who notices.
gender.female: You tip the attendant before asking about the guest who left without collecting. Tipping first is always more productive.

Coffee Bar.default: In-office caffeine and the conversation that flows around it. The most honest exchanges of the working day happen at the coffee bar.

gender.male: You make a pour and stay. The bar is a social waypoint. Everyone comes through.
gender.female: You arrive with a question and leave with three answers you didn't ask for.

Coffee Shop.default: Somewhere between office and home for people who don't want to be in either. The coffee shop holds conversations that belong in neither.

gender.male: You order and occupy. A man with a coffee and a newspaper is the city's most reliable invisible person.
gender.female: You sit near the window and let the light work for you. People gravitate toward the light. So do their secrets.

Coffee Station.default: The hub of the floor. Everyone passes through it. Everything worth knowing gets said within earshot of the coffee station.

gender.male: You refill a mug you've been carrying for twenty minutes and catch the conversation you came for.
gender.female: You make the coffee for the person next to you. Small acts of consideration generate large amounts of candour.

Cold Brew Station.default: Slow-steeped and unhurried. There's a patience to cold brew that the clientele doesn't share. You do.

gender.male: You wait for the pour with the calm of someone who has nowhere to be. You have somewhere to be. You don't show it.
gender.female: You ask about the blend. The barista talks for three minutes. At minute two they mention the regular who hasn't been in.

Cold Storage.default: Controlled temperature and the quiet preservation of things that can't afford to change. What's being kept here that couldn't wait for room temperature?

gender.male: You open the door and step in. The inventory list on the clipboard doesn't match the shelves.
gender.female: You photograph the storage log. Something came in recently that the log describes in terms that don't match the weight.

Color Bar.default: Hair colour and the chemistry of transformation. The salon's colour bar is where the most personal conversations happen.

gender.male: You wait for a consultation and learn more about the clientele than the price list.
gender.female: You sit in the chair and let the colourist work. Forty minutes at a colour bar generates a complete social map of the salon.

Command Center.default: Every feed, every frequency, every incident in the city flows through this room. The command centre knows what happened before anyone files the report.

gender.male: You read the board before anyone offers to explain it. You already know what half of it means.
gender.female: You ask the dispatcher a specific question about the relevant time window. The answer is on three screens at once.

Commercial Kitchen.default: Industrial food production and the organised chaos of a service that can't pause. The kitchen sees everything the front of house never does.

gender.male: You enter through the back and look like you know your station. The kitchen is too busy to check.
gender.female: You speak to the head chef between services. Post-service is the only moment the kitchen is honest.

Commercial Washers.default: Industrial-grade cleaning in volume and on schedule. What goes through these machines gets clean. What was on it before is the question.

gender.male: You check the wash log. Cycle time, temperature, load weight. One log entry doesn't match standard linen.
gender.female: You ask the laundry supervisor about non-standard loads. They describe one from forty-eight hours ago.

Common Patio.default: Shared outdoor space and the informal governance of proximity. Everyone on the common patio heard everything.

gender.male: You take a chair and a coffee and let the patio do the surveillance work.
gender.female: You join the group already there and within minutes you're part of the conversation you came to hear.

Community Board.default: Notices and announcements and the neighbourhood's public record of its own concerns. Read it carefully and it tells you what people are afraid of.

gender.male: You read every notice twice. The one that was recently removed left a rectangle of unfaded paint.
gender.female: You photograph the board and enlarge the corner notice that's been partially covered by the new one.

Community Room.default: A shared space used by enough people that nobody knows who belongs there. The best kind of room to be in without explanation.

gender.male: You set up at the table with a folder and look like you're waiting for someone who's late.
gender.female: You arrive early for the meeting and start talking to whoever arrives first. They always know something.

Computer Lab.default: Shared terminals and the browsing history of people who thought the session cleared when they logged out. It cleared for them. Not for you.

gender.male: You sign in under the guest account and check the network logs. Guest accounts see more than administrators intend.
gender.female: You use the terminal next to the one you're interested in and note the recent files sidebar.

Computer Labs.default: Multiple machines, multiple users, and the aggregate history of what a building's population searches for when they think they're alone.

gender.male: You walk the row of machines and read what's been left open. People always leave something open.
gender.female: You speak to the lab monitor about a technical question. While they answer, you read the activity log on the nearest terminal.

Concession Stand.default: Overpriced snacks and the last honest conversation before the main event starts. People talk at concession stands.

gender.male: You queue and listen. The line is slow and people fill the silence.
gender.female: You work the counter briefly under a volunteer pretext. From behind the stand you see everything.

Concession Stands.default: Multiple points of sale and multiple conversations happening simultaneously. You can't cover all of them. You pick the one that matters.

gender.male: You position yourself between two stands and catch fragments from both.
gender.female: You identify the stand with the longest queue. People waiting talk more than people moving.

Concierge.default: The building's human directory. The concierge knows who comes and goes, what time they arrive, and which requests they make at unusual hours.

gender.male: You tip before you ask. The concierge becomes the most cooperative source in the building.
gender.female: You ask for a recommendation and get a conversation. By the end of it you know the floor layout and three notable guests.

Concierge Service.default: Discreet, skilled, and trained to remember preferences without being asked. The concierge service has a better memory than the security cameras.

gender.male: You describe the person you're looking for in terms of their habits. The concierge has it in thirty seconds.
gender.female: You request the same service the person you're tracking always requests. The concierge volunteers the comparison.

Conference Center.default: Booked rooms and NDAs and the particular tension of people making decisions in borrowed spaces.

gender.male: You arrive with a name badge from a different event and walk every floor.
gender.female: You check the conference schedule at the front desk and ask which rooms are running late. Late rooms are the ones with problems.

Conference Room.default: A closed door with windows that people forget don't block sound from the corridor.

gender.male: You take the seat outside and review a document you don't need to review.
gender.female: You deliver a message to the wrong room and hold the door just long enough.

Conference Rooms.default: Multiple closed sessions running in parallel. The building's most consequential business happening simultaneously and mostly off the record.

gender.male: You check the booking system at the front desk for today's schedule. Three rooms aren't in the system.
gender.female: You walk the corridor slowly during a transition. What spills from open doors between sessions is unguarded.

Consultation Office.default: Private conversation between professional and client. The confidentiality cuts both ways — and you're about to test which way it cuts today.

gender.male: You schedule a consultation to get in the room. Getting in the room is usually enough.
gender.female: You're direct about what you need from the consultation. Direct requests get direct answers here more often than elsewhere.

Consultation Room.default: A room designed for difficult conversations. Whatever was said in here, it was difficult.

gender.male: You check the notepad on the table. The top sheet is gone. The impression isn't.
gender.female: You look at the chair arrangement. Who sat where tells you something about how the conversation went.

Convenience Store.default: Open late, no questions asked, and a security feed that covers every square foot of the floor. The store sees everything.

gender.male: You buy something you don't need and chat with the clerk. Clerks on overnight shifts tell you things.
gender.female: You ask the clerk about the regular who was in last night. They remember the regulars better than their own family.

Conveyor System.default: Automated movement of product through a space. Whatever's been diverted from this line left a gap in the count.

gender.male: You walk the line from intake to output. The discrepancy is at the second junction.
gender.female: You ask the floor supervisor about throughput variance. They explain the numbers and accidentally explain the problem.

Cooling System.default: The temperature management of a building that can't afford to let something get warm. What's in there that needs to stay cold?

gender.male: You read the system panel. The load is higher than the building's declared use would require.
gender.female: You request the maintenance log from the super. The cooling system has been serviced three times this month.

Cosmetics Section.default: Premium product and the social performance of appearance. The cosmetics section is where people talk while they try things they haven't decided on yet.

gender.male: You browse adjacent to the section and listen to the consultant's pitch. It's covering something.
gender.female: You ask for a consultation. In twenty minutes you've covered the floor and heard enough.

Council Chambers.default: The room where the city decides things publicly that it already decided privately. The chambers are theatre. The back corridor is where the business is done.

gender.male: You take a seat in the public gallery and watch who watches who.
gender.female: You introduce yourself as a constituent with a concern. Council members talk more to constituents than to investigators.

Counseling Center.default: Confidential sessions and the records that summarise them. Something in this building's clinical file tells the story you need.

gender.male: You present credentials that are technically sufficient. The receptionist calls to verify. While they call, you read the waiting room.
gender.female: You enquire about intake procedures. The counsellor explains more than the procedure requires.

Counselor Office.default: One chair for the counsellor, one for the client, and a file drawer between them full of what the second chair said.

gender.male: You knock and find the office empty. The notepad on the desk has an impression from the last session.
gender.female: You schedule the appointment under the guise of a routine matter. The counsellor takes notes. So do you.

Courtrooms.default: Public record in session. Every word spoken here is transcribed and eventually available. The transcript is what you need.

gender.male: You sit in the gallery for the case you didn't come to watch and look for the faces you did.
gender.female: You request the public transcript. The clerk tells you how long it'll take. You ask if today's session is on record. It is.

Courtyard.default: Open space enclosed by the building. A courtyard is watched from above by every window that faces it. And by you from ground level.

gender.male: You use the courtyard as a cut-through and note every window with a view of it.
gender.female: You take the bench at the centre and let the building look at you. You look back, and harder.

Covered Walkway.default: Shelter from the weather and from observation. The covered walkway is the path people take when they don't want to be seen taking a path.

gender.male: You walk the length at half pace. The walkway connects two buildings that officially have no connection.
gender.female: You note the cameras on the walkway and the gaps between them. The gap at the second pillar is three metres wide.

Craft Room.default: Scissors, glue, and the particular candour of people doing something with their hands while their mouths run unsupervised.

gender.male: You sit at the table and work at something slowly. The room talks around you within ten minutes.
gender.female: You join the group and ask what they're making. The conversation that follows covers considerably more than the project.

Cross-Dock Area.default: Product in, product out, minimal storage time, maximum movement. The cross-dock is where things disappear before they're ever recorded as arriving.

gender.male: You study the manifests at both ends of the dock. The numbers only match if you're not looking carefully.
gender.female: You ask the dock supervisor about the most recent non-standard transfer. The description doesn't match any legitimate category.

Currency Counter.default: Machines that count money faster and more accurately than the people depositing it. That precision is its own kind of evidence.

gender.male: You watch the output tape. The total doesn't match the deposit slip.
gender.female: You ask the teller to explain the variance. They reach for the manager instead of the answer.

Customer Lounge.default: Comfortable seating and the extended wait that makes people talkative. You're very comfortable waiting.

gender.male: You settle in and project the patience of a man who has nowhere to be. The lounge fills around you.
gender.female: You take the chair nearest the door and strike up conversation with the person who's been waiting longest.

Customer Parking.default: A lot that logs plate numbers and transaction times. What came and went here tells you more than the front desk will.

gender.male: You note which vehicles have been here since the lot opened. Two of them are on your list.
gender.female: You check the parking validation stamps. The timing on two transactions doesn't match the business hours on the door.

Customer Service.default: The front line between a business and the people it disappointed. Complaint logs are a map of everything that went wrong and who was involved.

gender.male: You present a complaint that gets you to a supervisor. Supervisors have more access than the first line.
gender.female: You ask calmly and specifically. Customer service responds to clarity in a way they don't respond to authority.

DJ Booth.default: Elevated and isolated with a view of the whole floor. The DJ sees everything. They just charge more to remember it.

gender.male: You get up to the booth under the pretence of a request. From up here the entire room is readable.
gender.female: You hand over a request slip with something written on the back. The DJ reads it and looks up.

DVD Collection.default: Physical media in a streaming age. The titles still here tell you what got used and what got borrowed and never came back.

gender.male: You check the checkout log. The same title has been borrowed four times by four different cards.
gender.female: You ask the librarian about the most frequently requested titles this past month. One of them is not a genre film.

Dance Floor.default: The performance space of a room where everyone pretends to be watching the music and nobody pretends to be watching each other. You watch everyone.

gender.male: You position yourself at the edge and read the floor from a fixed point.
gender.female: You move through the floor and come out with everything you needed in the time it takes for one song.

Dance Studio.default: Mirrors and movement and the discipline of controlled bodies. The one thing you can't hide in a room full of mirrors is your reflection.

gender.male: You observe from the corridor. Studio sessions are focused. The students don't notice the door.
gender.female: You join the warm-up and use the mirrors to watch the whole room without turning around.

Data Center.default: The physical infrastructure of information. Every server in this room is evidence. You just need a rack number and a warrant. Or neither.

gender.male: You badge in with a card that shouldn't work. It does. That's a problem for the security team. An opportunity for you.
gender.female: You present yourself as a vendor representative for the cooling system. Vendor access is broader than most people realise.

Day Care.default: Morning drop-offs and the routine of parents handing over the most important thing they have. That routine can be read.

gender.male: You arrive at drop-off and observe who's not following the expected pattern.
gender.female: You speak with the key worker. Their concern about one child's recent change is the thread you pull.

Daycare.default: Small children, watchful staff, and the daily exchange of information between parents who trust this place. That trust is leverage.

gender.male: You sign the visitor log and use your eight minutes of supervised access carefully.
gender.female: You speak to the director about enrollment procedures. In ten minutes you've seen the full facility.

Decontamination Room.default: A room designed to make things clean before they enter somewhere important. What came through here that required this level of thoroughness?

gender.male: You check the protocol log. The last full decontamination cycle was triggered by something not in the standard category.
gender.female: You review the contamination threshold records. One entry was manually overridden. That override has a signature.

Deli Counter.default: The social hub of any market. Conversation at the deli counter is as regular as the lunch rush.

gender.male: You order and wait. The wait is where the conversations happen and you make sure to be in one.
gender.female: You ask the server's recommendation. Servers who make recommendations talk. They tell you about the regular who ordered the same thing every Thursday until two weeks ago.

Delivery Area.default: The back entrance that everyone uses and no one supervises. What came in through here that didn't go in through the front?

gender.male: You walk the delivery area with a clipboard. Nobody questions a clipboard near a loading bay.
gender.female: You ask the receiving clerk to cross-check a delivery. The discrepancy you point to makes them check the rest of the manifest.

Delivery Station.default: Packages sorted and staged for the last mile. Every address on this station is a destination. One of them is not what it appears.

gender.male: You read the address labels in the unsorted stack. One of them matches a location you know.
gender.female: You ask about a missing package under a real-enough address. The station manager's search reveals the routing history.

Dental Chairs.default: The reclined position of vulnerability. Nobody lies in a dental chair voluntarily about anything. It's the one room where the truth gets easier.

gender.male: You sit in the waiting-area chair adjacent to the treatment bay and listen to the muffled consultation.
gender.female: You make the appointment and use the waiting time to ask the receptionist about a specific patient's last visit.

Dental Suite.default: A private clinical space where the conversation has nowhere to go but honest. Whatever was discussed in this suite mattered.

gender.male: You review the appointment schedule at the front desk under the guise of rescheduling.
gender.female: You ask the hygienist about the last time a specific patient was seen. The hygienist answers and adds detail.

Department Store.default: Multiple floors of merchandise and the staff who see every customer who passes through. The floor staff remember more than their job description suggests.

gender.male: You approach the floor manager under the pretence of a product query and redirect the conversation.
gender.female: You ask the floor assistant for help finding something specific. They walk you through a section you needed access to.

Dine-In Options.default: Sit-down service means time. Time means conversation. Conversation means something is being said that you want to hear.

gender.male: You request a table with a view of the room and spend an hour letting it come to you.
gender.female: You ask the server for a recommendation and use the exchange to position yourself for what comes next.

Dining Hall.default: Mass catering and the particular democracy of shared tables. Everything spoken at a communal table carries.

gender.male: You take the end seat at the middle table. The middle table hears both ends of the room.
gender.female: You sit down opposite someone eating alone. Shared meals create fast trust. You use that.

Directory Kiosk.default: The building's official map of itself. What's not listed on the directory is sometimes more useful than what is.

gender.male: You look for the floor that doesn't appear on the directory. It's there in the gap between floors eight and ten.
gender.female: You photograph the directory before anyone notices. Some omissions are more informative than inclusions.

Dispatch Center.default: The brain of the operation. Every resource, every unit, every call comes through here. So does everything it's trying to suppress.

gender.male: You read the call board like it's a case map. It is.
gender.female: You ask the dispatcher to replay the radio log for a specific window. They do. The gap in coverage is deliberate.

Dispatch Office.default: Schedules and routes and the coordination of movement. Something moved off-route. The dispatch office knows.

gender.male: You review the run sheets with the authority of someone conducting an audit. You are, in a way.
gender.female: You ask the dispatcher what changed on Tuesday's roster. The change wasn't in the system. It was verbal.

Display Windows.default: The store's public face and the first impression it chooses. What the window shows tells you what it wants you to see, which is useful for working out what it doesn't.

gender.male: You study the reflection in the glass as much as the display. The street at your back matters.
gender.female: You photograph the window from across the street. The interior arrangement visible through it tells you who's inside.

District Attorney.default: The law's front line against organised crime — or organised crime's best cover, depending on who the DA is. You're here to find out which.

gender.male: You present your case clearly and watch the DA's reaction to the name you mention.
gender.female: You request a meeting and come prepared. The DA doesn't dismiss people who come prepared.

Document Storage.default: Paper and boxes and the institutional compulsion to keep records of everything that happened. Everything includes the things they'd rather forget.

gender.male: You find the box by date and cross-reference it against the access log. One file was pulled and not returned.
gender.female: You ask the archivist for records under a broad but legitimate category. The pull includes the document you needed.

Dog Park.default: Off-lead dogs and on-lead conversations. Dog owners talk freely. Their dogs don't filter anything either.

gender.male: You sit at the bench and watch the pack dynamics. The humans aren't much different.
gender.female: You strike up conversation with the regular who's there every morning. They tell you about the car that parks and watches.

Dog Run.default: A bounded space for unrestricted movement. What the animals do tells you something. What the owners do tells you more.

gender.male: You use the perimeter bench as a fixed observation point. Good sight lines on three sides.
gender.female: You bring a dog biscuit you happened to have and introduce yourself to the group. You're included in the next conversation immediately.

Dog Ward.default: Canine patients and the staff who manage them with the careful attention that only animals who can't lie receive.

gender.male: You ask about a specific animal by description. The vet nurse checks the intake log.
gender.female: You spend a minute with the animals before asking your question. People who are good with animals get trusted faster.

Dog Wash.default: Self-service cleaning and the unhurried conversations of owners who came for the same forty-minute window.

gender.male: You use the station and listen to the owner next to you. They've been coming here for three years and noticed the change last week.
gender.female: You help someone with an uncooperative dog and earn a conversation that covers everything you needed.

Dolby Atmos.default: Immersive sound that makes the world outside disappear. Everyone in the theatre is fully focused on the screen and fully unaware of you.

gender.male: You stay near the exit during the big scene and watch who else isn't watching.
gender.female: You sit three rows back from the front. Front-row viewers never look behind them.

Doorman.default: The first and last person in this building to see everyone who enters or leaves. The doorman's memory is the building's real security log.

gender.male: You tip and ask. The doorman has been watching this door for eleven years and has decided you're worth answering.
gender.female: You greet them by name — it's on the tag — and ask your question as though you already know the answer. You get it.

Drama Theater.default: A stage, a lighting rig, and the particular permission of a performance space to be someone else. You use that permission.

gender.male: You arrive at rehearsal under the guise of a tech question. The drama department talks during rehearsal breaks.
gender.female: You take a seat in the house and watch the rehearsal. Directors notice attentive observers. You get five minutes of undivided access.

Drive-Through.default: Frictionless transactions and a camera on every lane. Someone drove through here at a specific time that matters to the case.

gender.male: You ask the manager about the CCTV coverage on the drive-through. The camera is positioned more precisely than the average franchise requires.
gender.female: You ask a simple question from the window and the server tells you the shift supervisor is the one to speak to. Good.

Drive-Through Lane.default: A fixed path between point A and point B with cameras at both ends. Everyone who uses it is logged.

gender.male: You queue in the lane and study the sequence of other vehicles. Pattern breaks are the point.
gender.female: You note the timestamp on your receipt. You triangulate it against the incident window.

Drive-Through Window.default: Thirty seconds of face time and a receipt that records what you ordered and when. That's more than most surveillance produces.

gender.male: You make a specific order at a specific time and verify the timestamp on your receipt matches the system.
gender.female: You chat with the server and ask who was on this window yesterday evening. They check the schedule without asking why.

Driver Facilities.default: Rest areas and amenities for the people who move everything the city consumes. Drivers know routes, loads, and what they were told not to notice.

gender.male: You buy a coffee in the driver lounge and sit down. You're accepted as one of them without a word.
gender.female: You ask for the operations manager. Drivers stop and look. You use the thirty seconds of distraction.

Drop-Off Service.default: Garments or packages surrendered and logged. The log has a name attached. That name interests you.

gender.male: You ask about a drop-off from earlier in the week. The counter staff checks and reads the name aloud.
gender.female: You review the collection receipts under the pretext of a mismatch. The timestamp on one of them places someone at the wrong address.

Dry Cleaning.default: Chemicals and precision and the removal of stains that have a story behind them. The dry cleaner sees the story before they remove the evidence.

gender.male: You ask the cleaner about a specific garment description. They remember it. Cleaners always remember the ones that were difficult.
gender.female: You drop off a jacket and ask while you're there about the spot-treatment protocols for organic material. The cleaner looks up.

Dryers.default: Heat and motion and the completion of a cycle. What went into the dryer, and whether it came out the same, that's the question.

gender.male: You open the drum before the cycle ends. Old habit. It tells you what you needed.
gender.female: You check the lint trap. What accumulates in a lint trap is a fabric record of what was cleaned.

EV Charger.default: A charging record tied to a vehicle and a time. Electric vehicles are quiet and logged. That combination works for you.

gender.male: You check the charger's session log. One vehicle was here for six hours at two in the morning.
